content.text: Why Citations Matter in RAG

In a standard language model interaction the user must trust the model's output at face value. RAG changes the epistemic contract: because every answer is supposed to be derived from retrieved documents, those documents can be surfaced as verifiable evidence. Citations turn a RAG response from an assertion into a claim with a paper trail. They let users validate answers against primary sources, let developers audit retrieval quality, and let compliance teams confirm that answers are grounded in authorised content rather than model parameters.
Inline Citation Format

The most common inline citation style is bracketed integers ([1], [2], ...) matching the numbering of the injected context chunks. The model is instructed to append a bracket after every factual claim. This pattern is token-efficient, easy to parse with a regex, and familiar to readers of academic text. Alternatives include superscript Unicode characters (¹²³), anchor tags for web rendering, and named identifiers ([smith2023], [api-docs:v3]) for document-centric corpora.

import re
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

CITE_SYSTEM = """You are a technical assistant. Answer from the numbered
excerpts only. After every factual claim, cite the excerpt number in
brackets, e.g. "HNSW uses a layered graph [1]." If a claim draws from
multiple excerpts, cite all: "... [1][3]". Never cite an index that does
not exist in the excerpts."""

cite_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", CITE_SYSTEM),
    ("human", "{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"),
])

def extract_cited_indices(answer: str) -> list[int]:
    """Parse all [N] citations from an answer string."""
    return sorted(set(int(m) for m in re.findall(r'\[(\d+)\]', answer)))

result = (cite_prompt | llm).invoke({
    "context": "\n\n".join(f"[{i+1}] {d.page_content}" for i, d in enumerate(docs)),
    "question": "How does HNSW maintain recall at high ef values?"
})

cited = extract_cited_indices(result.content)
print("Answer:", result.content)
print("Cited indices:", cited)
Resolving Citations to Source Metadata

After extracting cited indices, each integer is resolved to the source metadata stored on the corresponding Document object. LangChain Document objects carry a metadata dict that can hold any fields: source filename, URL, page number, section heading, author, or an internal document ID. The resolution step maps index N to docs[N-1].metadata and assembles a footnotes list for the response.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Citation:
    index: int
    source: str
    page: int | None
    url: str | None
    excerpt: str  # first 120 chars

def resolve_citations(
    cited_indices: list[int],
    docs: list,
    excerpt_chars: int = 120,
) -> list[Citation]:
    citations = []
    for idx in cited_indices:
        if idx < 1 or idx > len(docs):
            continue  # out-of-range — skip rather than raise
        doc = docs[idx - 1]
        meta = doc.metadata
        citations.append(Citation(
            index=idx,
            source=meta.get("source", "unknown"),
            page=meta.get("page"),
            url=meta.get("url"),
            excerpt=doc.page_content[:excerpt_chars].replace("\n", " "),
        ))
    return citations

# Usage
citations = resolve_citations(cited, docs)
for c in citations:
    print(f"[{c.index}] {c.source}  p.{c.page}  "{c.excerpt}..."")
Structured Citation Output with Pydantic

Rather than parsing citations from free text, you can ask the model to emit a structured object containing both the answer and an explicit list of cited source identifiers. This is more robust than regex parsing because the model's citation list is a first-class output field rather than an embedded convention in the prose.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from typing import List

class CitedAnswer(BaseModel):
    answer: str = Field(description="Answer text with inline [N] markers")
    citations: List[int] = Field(description="1-based indices of all cited excerpts")
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0)

parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=CitedAnswer)

STRUCTURED_CITE_SYSTEM = """Answer from the numbered excerpts only.
Include inline [N] citations in the answer text AND list all cited
indices in the citations array.
{format_instructions}"""

structured_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", STRUCTURED_CITE_SYSTEM),
    ("human", "{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"),
]).partial(format_instructions=parser.get_format_instructions())

chain = structured_prompt | llm | parser

result: CitedAnswer = chain.invoke({
    "context": "\n\n".join(f"[{i+1}] {d.page_content}" for i, d in enumerate(docs)),
    "question": "What ef_construction value is recommended for recall > 0.95?"
})

footnotes = resolve_citations(result.citations, docs)
print(result.answer)
for f in footnotes:
    print(f"  [{f.index}] {f.source}, p.{f.page}")
Citation Verification

Citation verification checks that each cited excerpt actually supports the sentence that cites it. There are two levels of verification: syntactic (the index is within range) and semantic (the excerpt entails or at least is consistent with the claim). Syntactic verification is free and should always run. Semantic verification requires either an NLI model pass or a second LLM judge call — add it when faithfulness requirements are high.
Rendering Citations in a UI

The final step is surfacing citations in the interface. Footnotes at the bottom of the answer (matching the inline [N] markers) work well for document-centric interfaces. For chat UIs, expandable source cards below the answer let users click [1] and see the exact excerpt, its source filename, page, and a link if available. This design pattern mirrors academic citation standards and builds user trust in the RAG system.

def render_markdown_with_citations(
    answer: str,
    citations: list[Citation],
) -> str:
    """Render answer + footnote block as Markdown."""
    lines = [answer, "", "---", "**Sources**", ""]
    for c in citations:
        parts = [f"[{c.index}]", c.source]
        if c.page is not None:
            parts.append(f"p. {c.page}")
        if c.url:
            parts.append(f"[link]({c.url})")
        lines.append("  ".join(parts))
        lines.append(f"> {c.excerpt}...")
        lines.append("")
    return "\n".join(lines)

md = render_markdown_with_citations(result.answer, footnotes)
print(md)
# Output example:
# HNSW achieves sub-linear query time [1] via hierarchical graph layers [2].
#
# ---
# **Sources**
# [1] ann-benchmarks.pdf  p. 4
# > HNSW query complexity scales as O(log n) for ...
#
# [2] hnsw-paper.pdf  p. 2
# > The algorithm builds M layers, each a subset of the graph ...
Attribution for Multi-Document Synthesis

When an answer synthesises information from several documents — for example, comparing two approaches described in different sources — attribution must track which claim came from which document at sentence or clause level. A useful pattern is to ask the model to emit a sentence-level citation map alongside the answer: a dict mapping each sentence index to a list of source indices. This is more granular than a flat citations list and allows downstream tools to re-generate the answer if one source is retracted or updated.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from typing import List, Dict

class SentenceCitationMap(BaseModel):
    sentences: List[str] = Field(description="Answer sentences in order")
    attribution: Dict[int, List[int]] = Field(
        description="sentence_index (0-based) -> list of cited doc indices (1-based)"
    )
    confidence: float

parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=SentenceCitationMap)

GRANULAR_CITE_SYSTEM = """Answer from the numbered excerpts. Split your
answer into individual sentences. For each sentence, record which excerpt
numbers support it in the attribution map.
{format_instructions}"""

granular_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", GRANULAR_CITE_SYSTEM),
    ("human", "{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"),
]).partial(format_instructions=parser.get_format_instructions())

result: SentenceCitationMap = (granular_prompt | llm | parser).invoke({
    "context": "\n\n".join(f"[{i+1}] {d.page_content}" for i, d in enumerate(docs)),
    "question": "Compare HNSW and IVF indexing strategies.",
})

for i, sentence in enumerate(result.sentences):
    sources = result.attribution.get(i, [])
    print(f"  {sentence} {sources}")
Handling No-Citation Responses

Models occasionally respond without any inline citations even when instructed to include them — usually on short, direct answers where the model treats the claim as too obvious to cite, or when the answer is a refusal. A post-generation check should detect zero-citation responses and either re-run the generation with a stricter prompt or flag the answer for human review. Refusals (INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT) are the one legitimate case where no citation is expected and should be handled separately.

import re

REFUSAL_PREFIX = "INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT"

def enforce_citations(
    answer: str,
    num_docs: int,
    chain,
    context: str,
    question: str,
    max_retries: int = 2,
) -> str:
    """Re-run generation if the answer has no valid citations."""
    if answer.startswith(REFUSAL_PREFIX):
        return answer  # refusals need no citation

    for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
        cited = re.findall(r'\[(\d+)\]', answer)
        valid = [c for c in cited if 1 <= int(c) <= num_docs]
        if valid:
            return answer  # citations present and in range

        if attempt == max_retries:
            break  # give up — surface with a warning flag

        # Retry with a more emphatic instruction
        strict_system = (
            "IMPORTANT: You MUST cite at least one excerpt number in brackets, "
            "e.g. [1], after every factual sentence. No uncited claims are allowed."
        )
        answer = chain.invoke({
            "context": context,
            "question": question,
            "extra_instruction": strict_system,
        }).content

    return f"[WARNING: no valid citations] {answer}"

safe_answer = enforce_citations(
    result.content, len(docs), chain, context_str, question
)
Citation Metadata Design at Ingest Time

The quality of rendered citations depends almost entirely on the metadata stored on Document objects during ingestion. A minimal metadata schema should include: source (filename or URL), page or section, a stable document_id for deep-linking, and an ingested_at timestamp for staleness detection. Richer schemas add author, version, access_level (for permission-filtered retrieval), and content_hash (to detect if a source document has been updated since ingestion).
Field
Type
Purpose
source
str
Filename or canonical URL — shown in footnote
page
int | None
Page number for PDF sources
document_id
str
Stable ID for deep-link to source in UI
content_hash
str
SHA-256 of chunk text — detect stale citations after re-ingest
access_level
str | None
Filter citations shown to authorised users only
Stale Citation Detection

A citation is stale when the source document has been updated since the chunk was ingested but the vector index still holds the old embedding. Stale citations are worse than no citations because they point users to content that may now contradict the answer. Two detection strategies: (1) store a content_hash on each chunk at ingest time and recheck it against the live source at query time — expensive but reliable; (2) store an ingested_at timestamp and surface a staleness warning when the source's last-modified date is newer than the ingest date — cheap and sufficient for most production systems.

import hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def compute_hash(text: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

# At ingest time — add hash and timestamp to metadata
def enrich_metadata(doc) -> dict:
    meta = dict(doc.metadata)
    meta["content_hash"] = compute_hash(doc.page_content)
    meta["ingested_at"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    return meta

# At query time — check for staleness
def is_stale(citation_meta: dict, source_last_modified: datetime) -> bool:
    ingested_at_str = citation_meta.get("ingested_at")
    if not ingested_at_str:
        return False  # no timestamp — assume fresh
    ingested_at = datetime.fromisoformat(ingested_at_str)
    return source_last_modified > ingested_at

# Example: surface a warning badge in the UI
for c in citations:
    last_mod = get_source_last_modified(c.source)  # from your file store / CMS
    if last_mod and is_stale(docs[c.index - 1].metadata, last_mod):
        print(f"[{c.index}] ⚠ Source updated since last ingest — citation may be stale")
Summary

Citations transform RAG answers from black-box assertions into auditable claims. The pipeline has five stages: inject numbered chunks, instruct the model to cite inline, extract and resolve cited indices to source metadata, verify citations syntactically (and optionally semantically), and render footnotes or source cards in the UI.
Number context chunks sequentially and include the same numbers in the citation instruction — this is the minimal setup that works reliably.
Use a Pydantic schema with a citations: List[int] field to get structured output rather than relying on regex parsing of free text.
Always run syntactic verification (index in range) before surfacing an answer; it is free and catches a common hallucination pattern.
Store rich metadata on every Document at ingest time (source, page, url) so citation resolution is informative at query time.
